items.q: In a school the ratio of boys to girls is 3:2. There are 30 boys. How many girls are there?
items.correct: 20 — each part of the ratio represents 10 pupils, so girls = 2 x 10.
items.misconception: There are 27 girls because the ratio 3:2 means subtracting 3 - 2 = 1 from 30 and adding to make 2 girls' part.
items.random_wrong: There are 60 girls because girls are double the boys.
items.why: Students treat ratios as additive (3 - 2 = 1 difference) rather than multiplicative, leading to subtraction-style errors.

items.correct: No — a prime number has exactly two distinct factors (1 and itself); 1 has only one factor.
items.misconception: Yes — 1 has no factors except itself, which makes it prime.
items.random_wrong: Yes — 1 is the smallest prime number.
items.why: Students apply a loose 'only divisible by 1 and itself' rule and miss the precise requirement of exactly two distinct factors.

items.misconception: 0.6 is larger because it has more digits.
items.random_wrong: 3/5 is larger because fractions are always smaller-looking but bigger.
items.why: Students don't fluently convert between fractions and decimals and compare them by surface features rather than value.
The answer examiners want is: 17. “15” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The answer examiners want is: 36. “12” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: For a fair die roll X, find E(X) (give to 1 dp).
items.why: Exact answer: 3.5. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
